walkthrough.title: Clear a Large Table
walkthrough.text: Let's get Twilight Imperium set up! This game needs a lot of space, so clear the biggest table you have. You need room for the galaxy map in the center, each player's faction sheet and cards on the sides, and the shared supply of tokens and decks. Plan for a long session because this game is an epic experience.

walkthrough.title: Place Mecatol Rex
walkthrough.text: Find the Mecatol Rex system tile and place it in the exact center of the table. Mecatol Rex is the galactic capital and the political heart of the game. Controlling it triggers the Agenda Phase and can score you victory points. Everything in the galaxy radiates outward from this central tile.

walkthrough.title: Choose and Place Factions
walkthrough.text: Each player selects a faction. For six players, deal faction sheets randomly or let players choose. Each faction has unique abilities, a unique flagship, starting technologies, and a home system tile. Place your home system tile at the edge of the galaxy in the position indicated by the setup rules for your player count.

walkthrough.title: Build the Galaxy
walkthrough.text: Now we build the map. Shuffle the remaining system tiles and deal a set to each player based on the setup guide. Players take turns placing tiles around Mecatol Rex following the rules in the Learn to Play guide. Tiles contain planets with resource and influence values, anomalies like asteroids and nebulae, and empty space.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Victory Point Track
walkthrough.text: Place the VP track board near the galaxy. Each player places a control token on the zero space. The game ends immediately when any player reaches ten victory points. VP is scored primarily through public and secret objectives, not through military conquest. Keep your eyes on this track at all times.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Objective Decks
walkthrough.text: Shuffle the Stage One public objective cards and place them face down near the VP track. Reveal two face up. These are the first shared goals everyone can work toward. Shuffle the Stage Two public objectives separately and set them aside. They come into play in later rounds.

walkthrough.title: Deal Secret Objectives
walkthrough.text: Shuffle the secret objective deck. Deal two secret objective cards to each player. Each player looks at both and keeps one, discarding the other face down. Your secret objective is a hidden personal goal worth one VP when scored. Do not reveal it until you score it.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Shared Decks
walkthrough.text: Shuffle the Action Card deck, the Agenda deck, and the planet card deck. Place them within reach of all players. Action cards provide tactical advantages during the game. Agenda cards are used during the political Agenda Phase. Planet cards represent the resources and influence of each planet.

walkthrough.title: Place Starting Units and Technologies
walkthrough.text: Each faction sheet shows your starting units, technologies, and planets. Place your starting plastic units on your home system tile as shown. Take your starting technology cards and planet cards. Your faction sheet is your reference for everything unique about your civilization.

walkthrough.title: Distribute Command Tokens
walkthrough.text: Each player receives their starting command tokens as shown on their faction sheet. Distribute them among your three pools: the Tactics pool for activating systems, the Fleet pool for determining maximum ships per system, and the Strategy pool for using secondary abilities of Strategy Cards. This allocation is a critical first decision.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Strategy Cards
walkthrough.text: Lay out the eight Strategy Cards numbered one through eight in the center where everyone can see them. These cards drive the game's turn structure. Each round, players will draft one Strategy Card each. The number determines turn order and each card has a unique powerful ability.

walkthrough.title: Determine the Speaker
walkthrough.text: Randomly determine the first Speaker and give them the Speaker token. The Speaker picks their Strategy Card first each round and breaks ties during the Agenda Phase. The Speaker role can change during the game through certain Strategy Cards and events. Being Speaker is a significant political advantage.

walkthrough.title: Place the Custodians Token
walkthrough.text: Place the Custodians token on Mecatol Rex. The first player to move units into the Mecatol Rex system and land ground forces on the planet removes this token and scores one VP. This also activates the Agenda Phase for all future rounds. Claiming Mecatol Rex early is a bold but rewarding move.

walkthrough.title: Review Your Faction
walkthrough.text: Before we start, take a few minutes to read your faction sheet thoroughly. Understand your unique abilities, your faction-specific technologies, and your promissory note. Each faction plays dramatically differently. Knowing your strengths and weaknesses before the first round will save you time and help you plan your opening moves.

summary.title: Clear a Large Table
bullets: Largest table available — room for galaxy + player areas
Plan for a long session (4-8 hours)

walkthrough.title: Round Structure Overview
walkthrough.text: Each round of Twilight Imperium has three or four phases. The Strategy Phase is where players draft Strategy Cards. The Action Phase is where all the gameplay happens. The Status Phase is where objectives are scored and the board resets. Once Mecatol Rex is claimed, the Agenda Phase adds political voting after the Status Phase.

walkthrough.title: The Strategy Phase
walkthrough.text: Starting with the Speaker and going clockwise, each player selects one Strategy Card. Each card is numbered one through eight. The number determines your turn order during the Action Phase, with lower numbers going first. Each card also has a unique primary ability that only you can use and a secondary ability that all other players may access.

walkthrough.title: Strategy Card Overview
walkthrough.text: The eight Strategy Cards are Leadership for gaining command tokens, Diplomacy for protecting systems, Politics for becoming Speaker and drawing action cards, Construction for building structures, Trade for replenishing commodities, Warfare for extra movement, Technology for researching new tech, and Imperial for scoring objectives and gaining VP.

walkthrough.title: The Action Phase: Taking Turns
walkthrough.text: Players take turns in Strategy Card number order, lowest first. On your turn, you perform exactly one action: a tactical action, a strategic action, a component action, or you pass. After each player takes one action, the cycle repeats. The phase ends when all players have passed.

walkthrough.title: Tactical Actions: Activate a System
walkthrough.text: The tactical action is the most common action and has multiple steps. First, place a command token from your Tactics pool on a system to activate it. Then move ships from adjacent systems into the activated system. If enemy ships are present, resolve space combat. Then invade planets with ground forces. Finally, produce new units at Space Docks in the system.

walkthrough.title: Movement Rules
walkthrough.text: Ships move from their current systems into the activated system. Each ship type has a movement value that determines how far it can travel. Ships cannot move through systems that contain other players' ships unless they are moving into the activated system for combat. Carriers and War Suns can transport fighters and ground forces.

walkthrough.title: Space Combat
walkthrough.text: When your ships enter a system with enemy ships, space combat occurs. Each ship rolls one die. A roll meeting or exceeding the ship's combat value scores a hit. The opponent assigns hits to their ships. Dreadnoughts, War Suns, flagships, and mechs can sustain one damage before being destroyed. Combat rounds repeat until one side retreats or is destroyed.

walkthrough.title: Ground Combat and Invasion
walkthrough.text: After space combat, if you have ground forces in the system, you can invade planets. Land your infantry and mechs on enemy planets. Ground combat works like space combat but with ground forces fighting. PDS units on the planet may fire at invading ships and ground forces. The winner takes control of the planet card.

walkthrough.title: Production
walkthrough.text: After combat, you may produce new units at any Space Dock in the activated system. Spend resources from your planet cards to build ships and ground forces. Each Space Dock has a production limit based on the planet's resource value plus two. Produced units are placed in the activated system.

walkthrough.title: Strategic Actions
walkthrough.text: Instead of a tactical action, you can use your Strategy Card's primary ability. This is your strategic action. After you resolve your primary ability, every other player in clockwise order may use the secondary ability by spending a command token from their Strategy pool. Secondary abilities are often weaker versions of the primary.

walkthrough.title: Command Tokens: The Action Economy
walkthrough.text: Command tokens are the game's most precious resource. Your Tactics pool determines how many systems you can activate each round. Your Fleet pool sets the maximum number of non-fighter ships allowed in each system. Your Strategy pool lets you use secondary abilities of other players' Strategy Cards. Balancing these three pools is a critical ongoing decision.

walkthrough.title: Resources and Influence
walkthrough.text: Every planet has a resource value and an influence value. Resources are spent to build units and research technology. Influence is spent to vote during the Agenda Phase. When you spend a planet's value, you exhaust the planet card by flipping it face down. Exhausted planets ready at the end of the Status Phase.

walkthrough.title: Technology Research
walkthrough.text: Technology is researched primarily through the Technology Strategy Card. To research a tech, you must own all prerequisite technologies shown on the card. There are four tech colors: blue for propulsion, red for warfare, yellow for cybernetics, and green for biotics. Unit upgrades make your ships and ground forces stronger.

walkthrough.title: Trade and Negotiation
walkthrough.text: Players can negotiate anything at any time. Trade goods, commodities, and promissory notes can be exchanged freely. Verbal promises are not binding, only the exchange of physical game components is enforceable. This means alliances are built on trust and mutual benefit. Negotiation is as important as military strategy.

walkthrough.title: The Status Phase
walkthrough.text: After all players pass in the Action Phase, the Status Phase begins. Each player may score one public objective and one secret objective if they meet the requirements. Then a new public objective is revealed. Players draw action cards, remove command tokens from the board, gain and redistribute two new command tokens, and ready exhausted cards.

walkthrough.title: Scoring Objectives
walkthrough.text: Objectives are the path to victory. Stage One public objectives are worth one VP each and have requirements like spending eight resources or controlling four planets outside your home system. Stage Two objectives are worth two VP with harder requirements. Secret objectives are worth one VP each with a maximum of three scored across the entire game.

walkthrough.title: The Agenda Phase
walkthrough.text: Once Mecatol Rex is claimed, the Agenda Phase occurs after every Status Phase. The Speaker reveals an agenda card, a galactic law or directive. Players discuss, negotiate, and threaten. Then each player votes using influence from their exhausted planet cards. The winning outcome takes effect. A second agenda is then resolved the same way.

walkthrough.title: Wormholes and Anomalies
walkthrough.text: Some systems contain wormholes that connect distant parts of the galaxy. All systems with the same wormhole type are considered adjacent. Anomalies include asteroid fields that block movement, nebulae that slow ships but boost defenders, gravity rifts that risk ship loss for extra movement, and supernovae that are completely impassable.

walkthrough.title: Promissory Notes
walkthrough.text: Each faction has a unique promissory note that can be traded to other players. When the holder plays the note, the effect occurs. For example, the Ceasefire note prevents attacks from the original owner for one round. Promissory notes are tradeable political currency that add depth to negotiations.

walkthrough.title: Winning the Game
walkthrough.text: The game ends immediately when a player reaches ten VP during the Status Phase. VP comes from Stage One objectives, Stage Two objectives, secret objectives, the Imperial Strategy Card, the Custodians token, agenda outcomes, and the Support for the Throne promissory note. You can only score one public and one secret objective per Status Phase.

walkthrough.title: Welcome to the Galaxy
walkthrough.text: Welcome to your first game of Twilight Imperium! We will walk through the first round with six players, each controlling a unique faction. The galaxy has been built, starting units are placed, and the Strategy Cards are ready. This is the dawn of a new galactic era. Let's begin with the Strategy Phase.

walkthrough.title: Strategy Phase: Drafting Cards
walkthrough.text: The Speaker picks first and takes Technology, card number seven. Going clockwise, players choose based on their plans. One player takes Trade to generate income. Another takes Warfare for military flexibility. The last player is stuck with whatever remains. The Imperial card, number eight, is critical because it lets you score objectives and gain VP for holding Mecatol Rex.

walkthrough.title: Action Phase: First Tactical Actions
walkthrough.text: The player with the lowest Strategy Card number goes first. They activate an adjacent system by placing a command token from their Tactics pool. They move a carrier with fighters and infantry into the system. No enemy ships are present, so they skip combat and land infantry on a planet, taking control of it and its planet card.

walkthrough.title: Expanding Into the Galaxy
walkthrough.text: Each player takes turns expanding outward from their home system. The first round is usually about claiming nearby planets for their resources and influence. Players move carriers loaded with infantry to unoccupied systems and take planets. Building a strong economic base in round one sets up everything that follows.

walkthrough.title: Using a Strategic Action
walkthrough.text: The Trade player uses their strategic action to activate the Trade Strategy Card. Their primary ability replenishes their commodities and establishes trade agreements with neighbors. Then every other player may use the secondary ability by spending a Strategy pool token. This is how the galactic economy starts flowing.

walkthrough.title: Beginner Mistake: Ignoring the Secondary
walkthrough.text: Player four did not spend a Strategy pool token when the Technology card was activated. They missed the secondary ability, which lets you research a technology at a discount. Secondaries are powerful, and skipping them because you want to conserve tokens is often a mistake in early rounds.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Use Secondaries Generously Early
walkthrough.text: In the first few rounds, spend Strategy pool tokens on important secondaries, especially Technology and Construction. An early technology can define your entire game. You will gain new command tokens during the Status Phase, so do not hoard them. The opportunity cost of missing a secondary is often higher than the token spent.

walkthrough.title: Approaching Mecatol Rex
walkthrough.text: Player two moves a fleet toward Mecatol Rex. Taking Mecatol Rex early is bold because it activates the Agenda Phase and scores one VP from the Custodians token. But it also makes you a target. Player two lands ground forces on Mecatol Rex, removes the Custodians token, and scores their first VP.

walkthrough.title: Negotiation Begins
walkthrough.text: Player three and player five share a border and start negotiating. Player three offers a trade agreement: they will exchange commodities and promise not to attack player five's border systems. Player five agrees and they shake hands. But remember, verbal promises are not binding. Only the exchange of game components is enforceable.

walkthrough.title: Beginner Mistake: Forgetting Objectives
walkthrough.text: Player six spent the entire round building a massive fleet and conquering planets, but they did not check the public objectives. The two revealed Stage One objectives are "spend eight resources" and "control four planets outside your home system." Player six has enough planets but forgot to keep resources unspent. Objectives are how you win, not fleets.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Always Plan Around Objectives
walkthrough.text: Before every round, look at the revealed public objectives and your secret objective. Build your entire strategy around scoring them. Military strength is only useful if it helps you score objectives. A player who conquers the galaxy but ignores objectives will lose to a player who quietly scores ten VP through careful planning.

walkthrough.title: The Status Phase
walkthrough.text: All players have passed. The Status Phase begins. Players check if they meet any objective requirements. The player who controls four planets outside their home scores a Stage One objective for one VP. A new public objective is revealed. Everyone draws action cards, removes command tokens from the board, and gains two new tokens to redistribute.

walkthrough.title: The First Agenda Phase
walkthrough.text: Since Mecatol Rex was claimed, the Agenda Phase now occurs. The Speaker reveals an agenda card. This one proposes electing a player to gain a special political title. Players discuss, negotiate, and spend influence to vote. The player with the most total influence from their planets has the most voting power.

walkthrough.title: Beginner Mistake: Exhausting All Planets Before Voting
walkthrough.text: Player one spent all their planet influence on building units during the Action Phase. When voting time came, they had no influence left to vote with. Always reserve some influence for the Agenda Phase, especially if a critical law is being voted on. Unspent influence is voting power.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Budget Influence for Voting
walkthrough.text: If the Agenda Phase is active, always keep some planets unexhausted going into it. Agendas can award VP, change game rules, or permanently disadvantage you. Having no votes means you have no voice in galactic politics. Budget your influence between spending and voting every round.

walkthrough.title: Round Two Preview
walkthrough.text: Round two begins with a new Strategy Phase. Players are now competing for territory and positioning for the next set of objectives. The player holding Mecatol Rex is a target but also in a strong scoring position. Alliances are forming, fleets are building, and the VP race is getting serious.

walkthrough.title: First Space Combat
walkthrough.text: In round two, player one moves a fleet into player three's system. Space combat begins. Player one has three cruisers. Player three has two destroyers and a carrier. Dice roll. Player one scores two hits. Player three scores one hit and uses their destroyer's anti-fighter barrage. After two rounds of combat, player one wins and takes the system.

walkthrough.title: Diplomacy in Action
walkthrough.text: After the battle, player three is upset and approaches player five for help. They negotiate a military alliance against player one. Player five agrees because player one is also near their border. This kind of dynamic alliance building is the heart of Twilight Imperium. The table is always shifting.

walkthrough.title: Watching the VP Track
walkthrough.text: By round three, the VP track shows two players at three VP, one at two, and three at one. The player who claimed Mecatol Rex and scored an objective is leading. The table starts coordinating to slow the leader. In Twilight Imperium, it is everyone's responsibility to prevent a runaway winner.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: Congratulations! You have played through the opening rounds of Twilight Imperium. You learned Strategy Card drafting, tactical expansion, the importance of objectives over military might, how negotiations shape the game, and why the Agenda Phase demands careful influence budgeting.

walkthrough.title: Ready to Conquer the Galaxy
walkthrough.text:
Before you continue your epic game, here are three things to remember.

First, check out General Tips for strategies on objective planning, negotiation, and command token allocation.

Second, reference the Appendix for a quick rundown of all Strategy Cards, unit types, tech trees, and key terms.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

summary.title: Ready to Conquer the Galaxy
bullets: Check out General Tips → objectives, negotiation, tokens
Reference the Appendix → Strategy Cards, units, tech, terms
Ask your personal GM Guide → tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Score Objectives, Not Kills
walkthrough.text: Military strength is a tool, not the goal. You win by scoring ten victory points through objectives, not by destroying opponents. Before committing to any military action, ask yourself: does this help me score an objective? If the answer is no, your resources are better spent elsewhere.

walkthrough.title: Expand Early, Fight Later
walkthrough.text: In round one, focus on claiming planets in your slice of the galaxy. Move carriers with infantry to nearby unoccupied systems and take planets for their resources and influence. Building a strong economic base in the first two rounds is far more valuable than early aggression against neighbors.

walkthrough.title: Negotiate Constantly
walkthrough.text: Twilight Imperium is a negotiation game as much as a strategy game. Trade commodities, offer ceasefires, make deals for Agenda votes, and build temporary alliances. A player who refuses to negotiate will be isolated and overwhelmed. Diplomacy keeps you safe and opens doors that military force alone cannot.

walkthrough.title: Watch the VP Track
walkthrough.text: Always know who is closest to ten VP. The player with the most VP is the biggest threat to everyone. If you let one player run away on the VP track without opposition, the game ends before you can catch up. Coordinate with other players to slow the leader through military pressure, Agenda votes, and denying objectives.

walkthrough.title: Pick Strategy Cards for Your Plan
walkthrough.text: Do not pick a Strategy Card just for its turn order number. Choose the card that best supports your objectives for this round. If you need technology, take Technology. If you hold Mecatol Rex and can score VP, take Imperial. If you need command tokens for a big military push, take Leadership.

walkthrough.title: Balance Command Token Allocation
walkthrough.text: Every time you gain command tokens, think carefully about where they go. More Tactics means more activations per round. More Fleet means larger fleets. More Strategy means more secondary ability access. Early in the game, invest in Strategy to use secondaries. Later, shift toward Tactics for more actions and Fleet for bigger fleets.

walkthrough.title: Choose a Focused Technology Path
walkthrough.text: Do not spread your technology research across all four color trees. Focus on one or two colors that support your faction's strengths. Most factions benefit from rushing a specific unit upgrade like Cruiser Two or Dreadnought Two. A focused tech path gets you powerful upgrades faster than a scattered approach.

walkthrough.title: Use Action Cards at Key Moments
walkthrough.text: Action cards provide tactical advantages like Sabotage to cancel opponent cards, Direct Hit to destroy damaged ships, and Skilled Retreat to escape combat. Hold your best action cards for moments when they have maximum impact. Playing Sabotage to cancel an enemy's critical Action Card can swing the entire game.

walkthrough.title: Plan Two Rounds Ahead
walkthrough.text: Look at the revealed public objectives and your secret objective. Plan not just this round but the next. If a Stage Two objective is revealed that requires sixteen resources, start building toward it now. If your secret objective requires destroying a flagship, start positioning your fleet. Advance planning is the difference between winning and losing.

walkthrough.title: Mecatol Rex Matters
walkthrough.text: Controlling Mecatol Rex triggers the Agenda Phase and enables VP through the Imperial Strategy Card. Holding it is powerful but makes you a target. If you take Mecatol, make sure you can defend it or that the political benefits outweigh the military cost. Do not ignore Mecatol, but do not overcommit either.

summary.title: Score Objectives, Not Kills
bullets: VP from objectives wins the game, not military conquest
Every action should serve an objective

summary.title: Expand Early, Fight Later
bullets: Round 1-2: claim planets for resources/influence
Economic base > early aggression

summary.title: Negotiate Constantly
bullets: Trade, ceasefire, vote-trade, alliance building
Isolation = death; diplomacy = survival

summary.title: Watch the VP Track
bullets: Leader is everyone's threat → coordinate to slow them
Don't let anyone run away unchecked

summary.title: Pick Strategy Cards for Your Plan
bullets: Match card to this round's objectives
Imperial critical when you hold Mecatol / can score

summary.title: Balance Command Token Allocation
bullets: Early: invest in Strategy for secondaries
Later: shift to Tactics (more actions) + Fleet (bigger fleets)

summary.title: Choose a Focused Technology Path
bullets: Focus 1-2 color trees matching faction strengths
Rush key unit upgrade (Cruiser II, Dreadnought II, etc.)

summary.title: Use Action Cards at Key Moments
bullets: Hold Sabotage, Direct Hit, Skilled Retreat for max impact
One well-timed card can swing the game

summary.title: Plan Two Rounds Ahead
bullets: Check revealed objectives → build toward them now
Secret objective positioning starts early

summary.title: Mecatol Rex Matters
bullets: Triggers Agenda + Imperial VP
Powerful but makes you a target; defend or trade politically

walkthrough.title: Objective Drafting and Timing
walkthrough.text: Advanced players plan their entire game around scoring objectives efficiently. Look at all revealed public objectives and your secret objective, then chart the fastest path to ten VP. Sometimes it is better to delay scoring an objective by one round to score two objectives simultaneously in a single Status Phase.

walkthrough.title: The Imperial Card Timing
walkthrough.text: Taking the Imperial Strategy Card at the perfect moment can swing multiple VP in one round. If you control Mecatol Rex and can score a public objective, Imperial gives you one VP for Mecatol plus allows an extra objective scoring. Timing this for a round where you meet multiple objectives is devastating.

walkthrough.title: Trade Good Economy
walkthrough.text: Trade goods are the most flexible resource in the game. They can be spent as resources or influence. Hoarding trade goods gives you a flexible reserve for critical moments, whether that is building a fleet, researching technology, or casting decisive votes in the Agenda Phase. Never underestimate the power of a large trade good reserve.

walkthrough.title: King-Making Awareness
walkthrough.text: In Twilight Imperium, every player has a responsibility to prevent a runaway winner. If you attack the leader, you might weaken yourself, but if nobody attacks the leader, everyone loses. Coordinate with other players to share the burden. The politics of king-making is the game's deepest strategic layer.

walkthrough.title: Political Capital in the Agenda Phase
walkthrough.text: The Agenda Phase is immensely powerful. Laws can permanently alter the game state, award or deny VP, and create new victory conditions. Build political alliances specifically for voting blocs. Save influence for critical agendas. Sometimes the Agenda Phase decides the game more than any battle ever could.

walkthrough.title: Slice Evaluation and Neighbor Assessment
walkthrough.text: During galaxy setup, evaluate which systems in your slice have the most valuable planets. Prioritize high-resource systems for early expansion. Also assess your neighbors. Are their factions aggressive or peaceful? Do their faction abilities threaten your position? Plan your defensive posture and diplomatic approach based on who sits next to you.

walkthrough.title: Action Card Management
walkthrough.text: Experienced players hold specific action cards for specific situations. Direct Hit is devastating against War Suns and Dreadnoughts that sustain damage. Skilled Retreat can save a fleet from an unwinnable battle. Sabotage cancels another player's critical action card. Managing your action card hand is a subtle but impactful skill.

walkthrough.title: The Support for the Throne Meta
walkthrough.text: Each faction's promissory note called Support for the Throne gives one VP to whoever holds it. Trading your Support to an ally early can cement an alliance and give them a VP boost. But it also means they can hold one VP hostage. Understanding when and how to trade Supports is an advanced political skill.

walkthrough.title: Faction-Specific Optimization
walkthrough.text: Each faction has an optimal opening and technology path. The Universities of Jol-Nar should rush technologies. The Barony of Letnev should invest in a powerful military. The Emirates of Hacan should leverage trade. Study your faction's unique abilities and build a strategy that maximizes their specific advantages rather than playing generically.

walkthrough.title: The Final Push
walkthrough.text: When you are two or three VP from winning, plan your final push carefully. You need to score those last points in a single round before the table can stop you. Prepare objectives, position your fleet, and build diplomatic cover. The winner is usually the player who creates an unstoppable two-round scoring sequence that the table cannot disrupt in time.

summary.title: Objective Drafting and Timing
bullets: Chart fastest path to 10 VP across all revealed objectives
Sometimes delay 1 round to score 2 objectives at once

Hoard for critical moments: fleet, tech, or Agenda votes

Prepare objectives, fleet, diplomatic cover simultaneously

entries.definition: The galactic capital at the center of the map. Controlling it triggers the Agenda Phase, enables VP through Imperial, and awards one VP for the Custodians token.
Each faction's starting system tile placed at the galaxy edge. Contains your starting planets and is the heart of your empire.
The primary scoring metric. First player to ten VP wins immediately. Scored through objectives, Imperial, Custodians, agendas, and promissory notes.
A shared goal worth one VP with moderate requirements like spending resources or controlling planets. Two are revealed at game start with more each round.
A shared goal worth two VP with harder requirements. Revealed in later rounds after Stage I objectives are depleted.
A hidden personal goal worth one VP. Each player keeps one from setup. Maximum three secret objectives can be scored across the entire game.
One of eight numbered cards drafted each round. Determines turn order and provides a unique primary ability for the holder and secondary ability for all other players.
Primary: gain three command tokens and spend influence for more. Secondary: spend influence to gain one command token.
Primary: choose a system containing your planet; opponents cannot activate it this round. Ready your exhausted planets there. Secondary: spend a token to ready two planets.
Primary: draw two action cards plus one for each player. Look at the top two agenda cards. Become the new Speaker. Secondary: spend a token to draw two action cards.
Primary: build a PDS or Space Dock on a planet you control without activating. Secondary: spend a token to build one structure.
Primary: replenish your commodities and establish trade agreements. Secondary: spend a token to replenish your commodities.
Primary: remove a command token from the board; then build in your home system. Secondary: spend a token to build in your home system.
Primary: research one technology, spending resources for prerequisites. Secondary: spend a token and resources to research one technology.
Primary: if you control Mecatol Rex, gain one VP. Score one public objective. Draw one secret objective. Secondary: spend a token to draw one secret objective.
Tokens distributed among three pools: Tactics (system activations), Fleet (ship limits), and Strategy (secondary abilities). The game's most precious resource.
Command tokens here are spent to activate systems during tactical actions. Each activation costs one token. More tokens means more actions per round.
Command tokens here determine the maximum number of non-fighter ships you can have in each system. More tokens means larger fleets.
Command tokens here are spent to use secondary abilities of other players' Strategy Cards. Running out means missing powerful secondaries.
A planet stat used to build units and research technology. Spent by exhausting planet cards. Higher resource planets are more valuable for production.
A planet stat used to vote during the Agenda Phase and sometimes to gain command tokens. Higher influence planets give more political power.
Universal currency that can be spent as either resources or influence. Gained through trade, commodities, and certain abilities. Extremely flexible.
Tokens that become trade goods when exchanged with another player. Replenished through the Trade Strategy Card. Only valuable once traded.
A cheap ship that does not count against fleet supply. Weak individually but effective in groups. Transported by carriers and War Suns.
Ground forces used to control planets. Required for invasion. Fight in ground combat. Transported by carriers.
A ship that transports fighters, infantry, and mechs. Essential for expansion and invasion. Moderate combat value.
A fast, cheap combat ship with the Anti-Fighter Barrage ability. Effective against fighter swarms.
A versatile mid-range ship with decent movement and combat values. Good for patrols and rapid deployment.
A powerful capital ship with Sustain Damage and Bombardment abilities. Can absorb one hit before being destroyed.
The most powerful ship in the game. Sustain Damage, Bombardment, and the ability to transport fighters and ground forces. Extremely expensive to build.
A unique capital ship specific to each faction. Each faction's flagship has different abilities and stats that complement their play style.
A structure placed on planets that can fire into adjacent space during combat (Space Cannon) and defend against ground invasion.
A structure on a planet that allows production of units. Production capacity equals the planet's resource value plus two.
A powerful ground force unit with Sustain Damage. Each faction's mech has unique abilities. Fights in ground combat alongside infantry.
An ability on Dreadnoughts, War Suns, Flagships, and Mechs that allows them to absorb one hit by becoming damaged instead of being destroyed.
An ability on Destroyers that fires at enemy fighters before the main space combat phase. Can thin out fighter screens.
An ability on Dreadnoughts and War Suns that fires at enemy ground forces on a planet before invasion. Can weaken defenders before landing troops.
A connection between distant systems. All systems with the same wormhole type (Alpha or Beta) are considered adjacent for movement purposes.
Special map features including asteroid fields (block movement), nebulae (slow ships, boost defenders), gravity rifts (risky but fast), and supernovae (impassable).
The first phase of each round where players draft Strategy Cards starting with the Speaker going clockwise.
The main phase where players take turns performing tactical actions, strategic actions, component actions, or passing. Ends when all players pass.
End-of-round scoring and cleanup. Score objectives, reveal new objective, draw action cards, remove tokens, gain new tokens, ready planets.
Political voting phase that occurs after the Status Phase once Mecatol Rex has been claimed. Two agenda cards are voted on using influence.
The player who picks their Strategy Card first and breaks ties during the Agenda Phase. A significant political advantage that can change during the game.
Placed on Mecatol Rex at game start. The first player to claim Mecatol Rex removes this token and scores one VP.
A faction-specific tradeable card representing a political promise. When the holder plays it, the effect occurs. Each faction's note is unique.
A promissory note that grants the holder one VP. Can be traded to cement alliances. The original owner can reclaim it if the holder plays it.
A card drawn during the Status Phase that provides tactical advantages like Sabotage, Direct Hit, or Skilled Retreat. Played at specific timing windows.
A card revealed during the Agenda Phase representing a galactic law or directive. Players vote on the outcome using influence from their planets.
A card representing a permanent upgrade across four color trees (Blue, Red, Yellow, Green) plus unit upgrades. Requires prerequisite technologies to research.
A card representing control of a planet, showing its resource value and influence value. Exhausted when spent, readied during the Status Phase.
